coredata.dc:description: The widespread use of lithium-ion batteries and the demand for high performance battery packs have made battery thermal modelling a crucial research area. This field helps to understand and manage the heat generated and the temperature distribution during battery operation. Effective thermal management is essential to ensure safety, extend battery lifespan and optimise efficiency. In this work, heat generation is identified as the primary driver of temperature change and distribution within the cell. Various battery models are reviewed and classified, driving the selection of the right model according to the application. Several thermal characterisation methods are described in detail, with a focus on experimental techniques that can be applied in-situ. Finally, the challenges posed by temperature distribution are discussed extensively, with a focus on how these challenges could shape the future research and design of the next generation of lithium-ion cells and battery packs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread use of lithium-ion batteries and the demand for high performance battery packs have made battery thermal modelling a crucial research area. This field helps to understand and manage the heat generated and the temperature distribution during battery operation. Effective thermal management is essential to ensure safety, extend battery lifespan and optimise efficiency. In this work, heat generation is identified as the primary driver of temperature change and distribution within the cell. Various battery models are reviewed and classified, driving the selection of the right model according to the application. Several thermal characterisation methods are described in detail, with a focus on experimental techniques that can be applied in-situ. Finally, the challenges posed by temperature distribution are discussed extensively, with a focus on how these challenges could shape the future research and design of the next generation of lithium-ion cells and battery packs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The primary cause of lithium-ion battery degradation is the occurrence of undesired side reactions that alter the chemical composition inside the cells, leading to a reduction in battery capacity. The reaction rate of such processes is highly dependent on battery temperature. Furthermore, the demand for fast and ultra-fast charging protocols enhances this problem, giving unacceptable temperature rise and leading to critical safety issues [1].

The Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a lithium battery in terms of temperature is typically from -20°C to 60°C, while the optimal battery temperature range is much more limited, going from 15°C to 35°C [2]. Indeed, at temperatures higher than 35°C, the degradation of the Solid Electrolyte Interface (SEI) occurs [3]. High temperature increases the loss of active material and accelerates the self-discharge, while, working at temperatures lower than 15°C, the electrolyte viscosity increases, the ionic conductivity decreases and the charge transfer resistance increases. Furthermore, at low temperatures, the rate of lithium plating increases involving an irreversible loss of active material that could also lead to dendrite formation and internal short circuits. In both cases, the battery capacity is reduced decreasing the battery pack performance [4].

An optimal battery thermal management prolongs the battery lifetime by minimising degradation from undesired side reactions, making accurate temperature modelling and monitoring of primary importance [5]. The temperature monitoring in battery packs is mainly implemented using temperature sensors applied on the surface of battery cells, as shown in Fig. 1. Such an approach presents several weaknesses. First of all, battery temperature measurement in commercial Battery Management Systems (BMS) is assigned to a reduced set of sensors shared among groups of cells because of the high cost of Analog to Digital Converters (ADC) [6]. The average sensors-to-cells ratio in many battery systems is about 1/10 [7]. Furthermore, surface sensors do not consent to measure cell core temperature, which may differ from surface temperature, or cell temperature distribution, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1. The measurement of core temperature sensor is important with high current rates and non-homogeneous temperature distributions [8]. In this way, the accurate temperature measurement of all the cells of a battery pack is challenging.

The need for reducing costs and high performances in battery systems is pushing for the use of large format cells and increasing the battery pack voltage beyond 800V [9]. Large battery format involves the reduction of hardware resources, and the use of high voltage will result in lighter cables, lower losses and faster charging speed for batteries [10]. However, the use of large cells increases the need for robust electric safety [11] and distributed thermal modelling [12].

In this context, battery modelling can be helpful for the estimation of temperature distribution within a battery pack, by monitoring electric parameters and understanding the main thermal properties of materials [13]. Thermal modelling and control of the battery should also be the starting point for the development of optimal charging strategies in order to minimise the electrically and thermally induced ageing effects [14–16]. Moreover, an accurate thermal supervision of the battery pack may improve the effectiveness of active cooling systems, especially in harsh conditions such as extreme fast charging [17].

The exponential increase in publishing scientific articles about developing a battery thermal model confirms that the attention on battery thermal management is gaining interest. Multiple studies describe several approaches for thermal parameter characterisation, but in some cases, these approaches are often material-oriented such as in the works of Yuksel et al. [18] and Shah et al. [19], and not appropriate for battery characterisation, or they require specific equipment that does not allow the characterisation outside a laboratory [20]. Other articles review the approaches for battery oriented thermal modelling. For instance, Akash et al. presented an interesting review of lithium battery temperature estimation strategies mainly oriented to integration on board BMS [13]. While this paper provides an overview of temperature estimation techniques, it misses a presentation of specific procedures for thermal model characterisation, which would enhance the applicability of the discussed methods.

The aim of this article is to present a wide set of techniques that help the reader in thermal modelling and characterisation of lithium-ion batteries. We go beyond the aforementioned review papers, writing not only an overview of thermal models but including also techniques for the determination of parameters, such as internal heat generation rates, and providing practical instruction and advice in thermal modelling and characterisation. Several procedures are shown in detail also with the help of flow charts describing the corresponding algorithm to implement. In such a way, the methods become ready-to-use tools for the reader. To the best of our knowledge, this has not been done before. The main topics covered are grouped in the scheme depicted in Fig. 2. We classify different families of thermal models, highlighting their advantages and limitations. We show in detail specific approaches for complete thermal characterisation of such models, with a focus on methods that can be applied in the place where the battery is working, namely in-situ. In some cases, they can be applied also during normal operation. In-situ characterisation is much more convenient than the so-called ex-situ one, which requires to dismount the battery pack and move it to a laboratory for testing. Furthermore, we analyse the thermophysical phenomena related to the main sources of heat generation and dissipation in lithium-ion batteries, offering a complete view of thermal energy distribution within a cell.

The paper is organised as follows. Section 2 shows the battery thermal energy balance explaining the source of the main contribution factors. In Section 3, we made a classification of the main thermal models used in the literature to estimate the temperature distribution and propagation within the battery, emphasising equivalent circuit models. In Section 4, we presented the main characterisation approaches

that could be used to estimate the thermal model parameters, giving importance to those approaches that could be potentially applied in-situ and online. In Section 5, we described the main processes that govern the heat transfer from the battery to the environment. Section 6 is dedicated to the analysis of the internal heat generation processes, while the seventh and Section 8 present some characterisation techniques for irreversible and reversible heat generation. Finally, the last two sections show the directions for future research trends and draw some conclusions.

Thermal models are mathematical representations that describe the heat transfer and temperature distributions within a battery. Such models are helpful for thermal management ensuring battery-safe operation, or to understand degradation phenomena under particular operating conditions.

The main heat generation, transfer and dissipation phenomena occurring in a battery are depicted in Fig. 3, where some variables are introduced:
q

Each of these components contributes to establish the heat balance within a battery that is described by the equation developed by Bernardi et al. [21]. In a compact way, we can represent the energy balance as:

represents the heat transfer rate due to the convection of the electrolyte inside the battery. The last term is typically considered negligible.

The main heat sources and dissipation phenomena inside a battery are listed in Fig. 4. Internal heat generation is ascribed to reversible and irreversible shares. Reversible heat generation mainly arises from electrochemical reactions occurring inside a battery that generate or absorb heat, depending on the reaction direction. Irreversible heat generation is caused by the Joule effect of electron flow, the ionic diffusion, and the ionic transport.
The heat dissipation is mainly due to the heat conducted, convected and radiated to the external environment, and it is easier to manage by using a battery thermal management system (BTMS).
Thermal models are used to describe the temperature distribution inside a battery and they are fundamental for temperature prediction and control, ensuring a safe working.

Battery thermal models may be classified considering several aspects, such as physical meanings, spatial dimensions and level of complexity. Fig. 5 shows a generalised classification for battery thermal models. First, battery thermal models can be classified into two main families: descriptive models, where the model reproduces the physical phenomena occurring in the battery; and behavioural models, which treat the battery as a black box reproducing only the main thermal behaviour. Second, these models can either be distributed models, where temperature and generated heat may vary continuously along the spatial dimensions or lumped models, where the battery is decomposed in finite elements with a reference average temperature value. Moreover, both distributed and lumped models could be zerodimensional (0D), or multi-dimensional (1D,2D,3D), which means that such models could contain the spatial information of temperature within the battery. Third, the relation between battery electrical and thermal models can be neglected or not, leading to a further model classification, correlated models or uncorrelated models. In the following, these classifications are approached more deeply.

Physics-based thermal models are the main representative models belonging to the descriptive family. In these models, some governing equations are used to compute the complete set of electrochemical and electro-thermal contributions for the energy balance equation [23]. Such equations are collected in Table 1.

Physics-based thermal models are typically included in a wider electro-thermal battery model such as pseudo-two-dimensional models (P2D), including both the electrical and thermal battery parameters [24].

Due to the complexity of these models, they are generally resolved with software for multiphysics simulations, like ANSYS and COMSOL. These software tools discretise the battery model in a Finite Element Model (FEM). It is a spatially highly resolved temperature distribution, which is calculated by using closed-form expressions for heat transfer and generation. As a result, the temperature distribution could be considered continuous for a macroscopic perspective [25].
All physics-based models require high computational capacity, which limits their applicability onboard BTMS [26].

Thermal models could be represented by an Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) where the node voltages represent the temperature of a particular point, while the current of every branch represents the heat flux that depends on the temperature (voltage) drop of the two nodes. The generation source is represented with a current source generator, the ambient temperature is represented using a voltage generator, and thermal resistances
R
th
and capacitances
C
th
of the equivalent circuit determine the temperature distribution along the cell.

Such models are mainly composed of thermal resistors
R
th
, which represent the resistance encountered by the heat flow through the battery, and thermal capacitors
C
th
, which represent the ability of a material or component to store thermal energy.

The most popular topology used for thermal modelling is the Cauer model, characterised by low computational burden and modularity. It is composed of an RC network with the thermal capacitors all connected to the thermal ground, and the thermal resistors that interconnect the capacitors, as it is depicted in Fig. 6 [25,28].

The elements in the Cauer model may have specific physical interpretations: the nodes’ potentials represent the temperatures of internal layers or particular locations within the system; the currents represent heat fluxes. Cauer models are particularly suitable for distributed thermal models thanks to the aforementioned properties [29].

Cauer ECMs can either be descriptive or behavioural thermal models. They are considered descriptive models when the circuit parameters are determined using the physical and geometrical information. Otherwise, they are behavioural models when the determination of the parameters is empirical.
Other kinds of ECMs could be obtained only with an empirical approach, fitting thermal data of the system, as in the case of Foster models.

Foster models are made of parallel RC loops connected in series, as depicted in Fig. 7. In these models, the resistances
R
th
k
and the capacitance
C
th
k
values are not linked to parts of the device, that is, they cannot be calculated from material constants and geometrical parameters of the device. Therefore, their values have no physical meaning. Moreover, a Foster RC model cannot be split or reused as a part of a modular thermal model. For this reason, it is less versatile than Cauer’s one. On the contrary, a Foster model is easy to fit with experimental transient data, and has lower computational complexity. For these reasons, sometimes a thermal model could first be implemented with a Foster model, and then transformed into Cauer’s one. This approach is successfully used for thermal problems in power electronics [30,31], and looks suitable for application to lithium-ion batteries.

The Lithium-ion battery behaviour is governed by various electrochemical processes, making the development of a model that balances complexity and accuracy of estimation a challenging task. Data-driven approaches could be helpful, without considering the nature of the physical processes that govern the battery. In such approaches, the only information used is a dataset of experimental data collected observing the response of the battery under different conditions. The methodology involves fitting an equivalent model that reproduces the battery behaviour. For this reason, data-driven models are the main representative models of the behavioural family. Data-driven models can be classified into statistical models and machine learning models.
Statistical models mainly employ regressions and optimisation techniques to adjust the parameters of a mathematical equation or ECMs in order to match the system behaviour [25,29,32].

Fig. 8 shows an example of statistical data-driven modelling approach based on battery impedance measurements used by Mattia et al. It consists of stimulating the battery with current signals to extract the battery impedance spectra at different temperatures. From the impedance spectra they selected the imaginary part at specific frequencies and they used it as an indicator for battery temperature by using an exponential relation. The model should be trained and finally validated over new measurements.

Statistical models are generally used in applications where the functions to model are almost linear [32] or at least monotonic [34], allowing for preliminary assumptions based on evidence. Such assumptions are not necessary for machine learning models, which are more effective for identifying specific patterns that may not be clearly visible in output variables.

Among machine learning models, the most used ones are those based on artificial Neural Networks (NN) that are very effective in non-linear modelling and in the identification of noisy patterns. For this reason, they are used in many research works for temperature prediction [35] or to support battery thermal models to enhance their performances [36].
Further data-driven approaches could be even used to support and optimise battery thermal models, such as random forest regressions [37] or Evolutionary Algorithms (EA) [38,39].

A battery thermal model could be lumped or distributed. In a distributed thermal model the inner temperature and heat generation rate change continuously through one or more spatial dimensions. In the case of one spatial dimension, the parameter distribution is described by Ordinary Differential Equations, while in the case of multiple dimensions we use Partial Differential Equations.

While a three-dimensional distributed thermal model provides a more accurate thermal distribution across the cell, it involves complicated physical equations in a spatial domain. Consequently, it is typically addressed using multiphysics simulators.

The 3D version of the energy balance Eq. (1) neglecting the internal convection of the electrolyte could be expressed in rectangular coordinates

ECM models such as Cauer thermal models should not be considered distributed thermal models because the model parameters do not change continuously along the three dimensions. Every RC element of the circuit represents the behaviour of an equivalent segment/area/volume that could be individuated in a particular location of the battery. ECM could be considered as a discretisation of the thermal parameters whose changes could be represented with a 1D, 2D or 3D matrix of concentrated spatial samples that we define as lumped elements.

A clear example is shown in the work of Haussmann et al. [40], where a 2D ECM is used to approximate the spatial temperature distribution of a prismatic Li-ion cell. Another example is the 3D lumped model shown in Fig. 9 used by Damay et al. in [41] to model a prismatic cell, that includes the radial thermal resistances from the core along x-y-z directions, the surface thermal resistances connecting all the surface faces, the thermal resistance of the metallic contacts and finally the thermal resistance of all the faces of the battery casing towards the ambient due to convection.
These models enable the acquisition of an approximated temperature distribution inside the cell while maintaining a low computational burden.

Even the heat generation rate could be represented in lumped elements, as shown in Fig. 10. This is useful when the heat generation rate arises from distributed sources that could be modelled with lumped elements along one or multiple directions.

Keil et al. use this approach in a 1D lumped ECM model to obtain the temperature distribution along the radial direction [29]. By applying the Kirchhoff’s current law, it is possible to obtain the state equations in a discrete-time domain (4),

The Cauer thermal model is modular and the thermal model of a battery can be extended with additional parameters used to model any cooling system of the battery pack. This approach is adopted in the work of Lebrouhi et al. [42], where they modelled a battery pack composed of twenty-four Li-ion cells with an integrated active cooling system composed of a phase change material with a liquid coolant circulation system. This model, shown in Fig. 11, remains a lumped thermal model where every component of the system is modelled with a concentrated RC element.

The low complexity of the lumped thermal model arises from the reduced set of parameters required to estimate the internal temperature. This aspect is particularly advantageous for onboard applications, where the thermal model is embedded in a broader electro-thermal model with a comprehensive view of the electrical and thermal parameters of the battery.
Foster thermal models could not be considered lumped thermal models because it does not provide a temperature distribution.

When the internal temperature gradients are small, we can model the battery with a single internal heat capacitance considering the internal temperature to be uniform
T = T
( t )
. This is defined as a zero-dimensional (0D) lumped thermal model.

We could assume a uniform internal temperature distribution as soon as:

th
int−surf
is the thermal resistance from the battery to the environment, and
Δ t =
t

−

is the time-step.
0D lumped thermal models are widely used for internal temperature battery estimation as confirmed by the literature [8,42].

The electrical and thermal models of a Li-ion battery are intrinsically correlated with each other. Temperature affects the interior electrochemical reaction processes that govern the output voltage of the battery. Parameters used in electrochemical models, such as diffusion coefficients and ionic conductivity [24], or discrete elements used in ECMs to model such electrochemical processes [46] may considerably change with temperature, while, the overpotential affects both reversible and irreversible components of the internal heat generator
Q
g
of the thermal model [45].

As an example, Fig. 13 illustrates the correlated electrothermal battery model used by Saqli et al. in which the thermal model requires the open-circuit voltage (
U
OC
), instantaneous battery voltage (
V
t
), and current ( I ) to determine internal heat generation. Meanwhile, the electrical model requires the temperature computed as the mean value between the core and surface temperatures to determine
U
OC
and the electrical parameters
R
0
,
R
1
,
R
2
,
C
1
, and
C
2
[25]

Such dependencies should be considered especially when working with high C-rates, where the cell overpotential is more pronounced [47]. Coupled battery models have enhanced prediction performances with very low estimation errors [25], the main drawback is the increase in model complexity, and the increasing need for computational performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complexity and application of thermal models
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 lists some thermal models used in recent papers. The models are ordered in terms of complexity, considering the computational burden needed for the model characterisation and temperature computation.
The computational burden is proportional to the number of elements of the battery space vector, while temperature computation is easier in linear models than nonlinear ones.

According to the aforementioned assertions 0D behavioural ECMs have the lowest complexity because the temperature state vector has only one element, the core temperature, therefore they are the most used for embedded applications. On the contrary, distributed thermal models and descriptive models may provide more precise temperature estimations, which are generally auspicated in laboratory simulations, accounting for the drawback of increasing the model complexity.

In some cases, different approaches are jointly used to study the heat transfer path, as in the case of Zhu et al. They used two approaches: a numerical simulation, and a thermal circuit of equivalent resistors. In this way, the thermal resistances associated with a reduced set of temperature nodes, corresponding to different battery sections, are optimised with numerical simulations. Such an approach improves the performances of a simplified thermal model reducing the simulation time and the computational burden [48].

The temperature distribution inside the battery depends mainly on the thermal conductivity κ of battery components and their arrangement. Heat can be stored and released in battery material, and this behaviour can be explained with a heat capacity
C
p
. One of the main tasks for the thermal characterisation of a battery consists of evaluating these two parameters appearing in the heat balancing Eq. (1).

This section describes these two parameters and surveys the thermal characterisation methods that can be used to determine them, focusing especially on those particularly suitable for lithium-ion batteries.

Thermal conductivity quantifies the material ability to transfer heat. Metals, inorganic, and crystalline materials typically have higher thermal conductivity coefficients than organic and non-crystalline materials. Indeed, these materials have a more ordered structure, where heat can flow easily. Porosity also affects thermal conductivity. Materials with larger pores have smaller thermal conductivity coefficients because the pores act as insulators, blocking the heat flow [50].

The heat generated by lithium-ion batteries can involve temperature gradients both inside and outside the battery. The main heat sources are in or near the electrolyte/separator region, so the thermal conductivity of the electrode materials within this region is a vital property for controlling internal temperature gradients [51].

Lithium-ion batteries are multi-composite objects with geometrically oriented material. Therefore, the thermal conductivity may change considerably in some directions. Thermal conductivity is a tensor and its geometrical components should be defined according to the structure of the battery. In prismatic and pouch cells, thermal conductivity is decomposed in
κ
x
,
κ
y
and
κ
z
, according to the length, height and thickness of the sample (2), while in cylindrical geometries it is more appropriate to decompose it in
κ
r
,
κ
ϕ
and
κ
z
, according to radial, angular and axial directions (3).

Thermal diffusivity α [
m

(8)

In Table 3 we reported some reference values for radial and axial battery thermal conductivity for some 18650 lithium cells with different cathode materials.

The specific heat capacity measures how much heat is required to raise the temperature of a given mass of material by 1 degree Celsius. The specific heat capacity of a lithium-ion battery is a key parameter for thermal investigations, but it is not typically provided by manufacturers. Therefore, it is important to determine the specific heat capacity of the battery before conducting any thermal analysis. In the case of lithium-ion batteries, the specific heat capacity is typically higher at higher temperatures. Moreover, the battery specific heat increases with the decreasing of State of Charge (SOC), because of the SOC dependence of the specific heat of the anode [57].

The average battery specific heat capacity
C
p
is extracted using the energy conservation equation [58,59]:
(13)
Q = m
C
p
Δ T
where Q is the heat generated by the battery, m is the mass of the battery and
Δ T
is the temperature rise. The total specific heat capacity of the battery can be calculated according to the density, volume and specific heat capacity of each material of the battery:
(14)
C
p
=
∑

where ρ and
ρ

are the density of the cell and constituent materials, respectively, and
c

and
v
k
are the specific heat capacity and volume of the constituent materials, respectively. This method is the most straightforward approach but requires accurate values for both the mass and specific heat capacity for all constituents in the cell [60].

In the literature, the specific heat capacity was measured within the temperature range of -20°C to 65°C, which corresponds to the operating window for lithium-ion cells. However, there is a lack of experimental data beyond 65°C, which is relevant for safety simulations at higher temperatures, or below -20°C, which is significant for military and aerospace applications. According to the research carried out by Steinhardt et al. cylindrical cells exhibit a lower specific heat capacity than other cell types. It is essential to consider parameters such as temperature, SOC, and design aspects like layer thickness, as they all influence the specific heat capacity [61].
In Table 3 we reported some reference values for heat capacity for some 18650 lithium cells with different cathode materials.
The thermal characterisation methods could be computational or experimental.

Computational methods are mainly used within FEM and computed with multiphysics software. The main drawback of these methods is that they require to know the thermodynamic properties of its components. This information can be obtained in two ways: either dismantling the battery and measuring experimentally the properties of each component [62], or having access to proprietary data from manufacturers. Since both ways are not easy to achieve, in some cases, researchers use average values collected from the literature [63], neglecting the variability that affects thermodynamic properties of batteries with different materials, internal structures and production processes [60].

Computational methods could be very efficient in the determination of the thermal parameters of lithium-ion battery components. The application of such approaches on a complete battery, rather than singular materials, should be contextualised and constrained, because different state conditions like SOC, State of Health and temperature may affect the thermal properties of the sample. Moreover, the properties of the battery layers may further change in the presence of the electrolyte and after the formation cycle [64]. In particular, the electrolyte has a huge influence on thermal conductivity, because it fills the air gaps between layers and some researchers found that it may increase the global thermal conductivity up to 50% [65].

Experimental methods include all the approaches that estimate the thermal parameters through experimental tests directly taken on the sample. They could be either material-oriented or battery-oriented, depending on the testing object. Material-oriented approaches are used to estimate the thermal parameters of the battery components, which are then used as inputs for computational approaches.
Battery-oriented experimental methods aim to obtain an estimation of the thermal properties of the overall battery that could directly be used in battery thermal models.

The experimental methods could be invasive or non-invasive, depending whether on they require or not structural changes to the battery, such as the insertion of sensors inside the battery [8], or dismantling the battery and extracting materials to characterise [62].

Experimental methods could be in-situ or ex-situ. In-situ approaches are generally non-invasive as they test the battery in the same place where it is operating. Conversely, ex-situ approaches require particular instrumentation and they should be performed in a laboratory taking out the battery from its test bed.

In-situ approaches are mainly preferred for engineering applications for their versatility. In some cases, they could be integrated onboard a BMS and performed periodically, tracking any parameter changes with ageing.

Table 4 collects all the characterisation methods used to identify thermal conductivity κ and heat capacity
C
p
, such methods are explained in detail in the rest of this section.
According to Yuksel et al. the thermal conductivity evaluation methods can be categorised into steady-state methods and transient methods [18].

Steady-state methods measure thermal conductivity when the material reaches a state of thermal equilibrium. Although this is the most accurate and mathematically simple, steady-state methods can take a long time, and the equipment to perform the test is often expensive due to the need for thermal insulation. Conversely, transient methods are faster than steady-state methods, because they measure thermal conductivity during the heating process. Furthermore, they require simpler equipment, such as probes or wires, but they are less accurate and can be affected by heat losses.

Among the steady-state methods, the guarded hot plate (GHP) and heat flow meters, are widely used. In Guarded hot plate method, the specimen is sandwiched between a hot and a cold plate arranged symmetrically. Thermal conductivity κ is calculated from the heat power rate and the temperature difference across the specimen [72]. Instead, by using heat flow meters, the material is placed between a heated plate and a cooled one, and the heat flow through the sample is measured with heat flux transducers [62,73].

Among transient approaches, it is worth mentioning laser flash method, where a laser pulse is used to heat one side of a sample, and the temperature rise on the opposite side is measured using optical temperature sensors [74].

The ex-situ approaches for specific heat capacity determination are mainly calorimetric techniques. These methods involve measuring the heat flow through the test object and observing the resulting temperature rise [75].

In the framework of battery research, the adiabatic calorimeter is a standard and appropriate method for the measure of specific heat capacity [60], but accelerating rate calorimeter [76] and differential scanning calorimetry are also commonly used [77].

Solving parameter optimisation problems can help in battery thermal model characterisation. These methods are used to fit simplified functions to experimental data by minimising the residual. This approach allows for the description of both electrical and thermal effects, and can be used for the identification of the heat capacity
C
p
and thermal transfer conductivities κ . [78]. These approaches are capturing the researcher’s interest thanks to their remarkable ability to measure multiple thermal parameters simultaneously [61].

These approaches have lower accuracy than the ex-situ techniques, but they are particularly interesting for their possibility to be implemented on board and online. The full set of parameters is calculated by solving optimisation problems, like in an inverse method that is schematically described in Fig. 14. It consists of a simplified 2D thermal model simulated simultaneously with a thermal experiment. Some temperature sensors are placed on the surface of the battery to characterise, and the temperatures at the location of the sensors are simulated using the 2D thermal model. The sum of the squared residuals between the measured and simulated temperatures are calculated as:
(15)
S =
( Y − T )
T
W
( Y − T )
where Y is the simulated temperature, T is the measured temperature and W is the weight matrix. Finally, an optimisation program adjusted the thermal parameters to minimise the sum of squared residuals S , and such optimal parameters are used for the thermal model. The parameters of the simulated model are determined minimising the difference between the output parameters coming from the simulated model and a real experiment [66].

Such an approach could potentially be used online considering the internal heat generation that normally occurs during battery cycling as the heat source. In this case, the internal heat generation should also be optimised, and the number of optimisation parameters increase, leading to a slighter more complex problem [68].

A pulsed current could be used as an internal heat source. Bryden et al. use a lumped thermal model for the battery thermal behaviour with an internal resistance composed of two series resistances,

A similar approach analyses the temperature resting trend after a discharge stage [70]. This method is suitable with a 0D model with a first-order resistive-capacitive model. In such a model, the temperature relaxation curve can be expressed by:

Ruan et al. proposed a novel experimental technique for thermal characterisation without any special thermal test equipment. A sinusoidal alternating current with the frequency of 50 Hz is used to heat the battery, and the temperature rise with the computed heat generation is used for an online estimation of the specific heat capacity and thermal resistance through a recursive instrument variable least square algorithm.

Transient measuring approaches are preferred for two main reasons: first, they do not require expensive equipment; second, to track thermal parameters during battery life thanks to the online implementation.
Battery heat dissipation is mainly ascribed to the thermal energy transferred from the battery to the external environment. It is made up of three components: heat conduction and heat convection.

Heat-conducting elements (HCE) are often used in BTMS to regulate the battery temperature. They are mainly composed of high thermal conductance materials with a liquid flowing inside, placed in contact with the battery. They could be used as heat sinks when the battery is too hot, or also as heat sources to warm up the battery if the temperature is too low.

The heat transfer from the battery to the HCE is described with the thermal contact resistance (TCR), whose magnitude mainly depends on the materials, the contact pressure, the surface roughness and the flatness of the contacting surfaces [79].

In correspondence of the contact surface between the battery and the HCE, there is a temperature discontinuity that is caused by TCR, as shown in Fig. 16. This temperature discontinuity is given by:

κ
g
is very low, therefore gaps introduce heat transfer resistance that contributes to temperature change across the interface. By using a filler conductance paste the interfacial thermal resistance can be reduced.
TCR determination is a quite complex problem because TCR is influenced by many factors, requiring a measure with experimental techniques [81].

Battery heat can also be transferred to electrical contacts through the battery terminals. In this case, heat flows parallel to the layers, taking advantage of the high conductance of the current collectors, before moving through the battery terminals and dissipating in the electrical contacts. Optimising the design of electrode tabs and caps can help reduce internal heat generation and improve temperature distribution throughout the cell [82].

For battery packs without HCE, heat is transferred to support structures and neighbouring cells via conduction. However, heat transfer is less efficient than the case with HCE, as the contact surface may be reduced and irregular. This makes it challenging to accurately estimate dissipated heat without employing multiphysics simulation software.

Using the battery without HCE, the heat is dissipated mainly by convection:

The heat generation in batteries during operation involves a temperature increase of the whole battery pack. High temperature may cause the degradation of capacity and lifespan, or even trigger catastrophic events like a thermal runaway in which the battery could explode. There are several phenomena causing the internal temperature rise of the battery. Some of them are correlated to the electric properties of materials, some others arise from battery ageing. These phenomena are depicted in Fig. 17, which schematically represents the internal structure of a lithium-ion battery.

The total internal generated heat can be written as the sum of all the power loss contributions, as follows:

The heat released or absorbed due to the entropy change of the system is known as reversible heat because nearly the same amount of entropic heat absorbed (or released) in the forward reaction is released (or absorbed) in the backward reaction. Reversible heat is generated by lithium intercalation that is the movement of lithium ions inside the electrodes causing reduction or oxidation of electrode materials.

In addition to the primary reactions at the electrodes, there are other reactions in the cell such as gas evolution, SEI formation, electrolyte-anode/electrolyte-cathode reactions and electrolyte-lithium metal reactions in the presence of lithium plating. At high temperatures, due to increased kinetics, a series of reactions can take place to generate a significant amount of heat, possibly leading to a thermal runaway. In general, the heat generated due to side reactions can be modelled as:
(31)

When the current is turned off, the concentration gradients relax, generating the heat of mixing. In commercial batteries, such heat is negligible, since batteries have good transport properties and limited concentration gradients [91]. However, if the heat of mixing is not considered, it is not possible to model the heat that is generated after the current is turned off. Furthermore, the heat of mixing contributes up to 20% to the total heat generation at a high charging rate, for the large gradients occurring [92]. Under the assumptions of constant temperature and pressure, the heat of mixing can be assumed as follows:
(32)

In summary, the two main contributions to heat generation are the irreversible and reversible heat (
Q
J
and
Q
Δ S
). The contribution of reversible heat to total heat generation cannot be neglected. Liu et al. in one of his papers assess that the reversible heat could be up to the 30% of the total generated heat and it is never less than 5% [94].

Current and temperature are the two main factors that influence the total heat generation. Heat generation increases with current, as both contributions depend on it. Specifically, reversible heat exhibits a linear dependence on current, while irreversible heat follows a quadratic dependence. Total heat generation also increases as temperature decreases. This occurs because lower temperatures increase internal battery resistance, leading to higher irreversible heat generation [94].

Besides the rise in temperature due to the generated heat, the temperature uniformity inside a battery becomes an important issue. Note that a rapid change in the battery temperature by a large external heating or cooling power leads to a large temperature gradient. So, the resulting battery temperature depends on the equilibrium between the temperature rise due to heat generation and the heat dissipated to the environment [95].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of the irreversible heat generation
ce:section.ce:para: Irreversible heat arises from the total heat generated in the electrodes, separators, and collectors, from electrochemical reaction and resistance to both fluxes of electrons and ions [47]. It could be formulated as the sum of four contributions: the irreversible electrochemical reaction heat; the ohmic ionic heat; the ohmic electronic heat; and the ionic migration heat [96].

Irreversible heat is not uniformly distributed within the battery. Several research papers state that there is a higher heat generation rate in the proximity of the positive terminal of the battery [34,47]. This behaviour is generally due to the high resistance of the positive tab used to connect the positive current collector to the positive terminal cap [97]. Moreover, the electrical conductivity of the aluminium used for the positive current collector is higher than the conductivity of the copper used for the negative current collector [98].

The irreversible heat generation could be modelled with a good approximation using a purely ohmic resistor which embodies the overall behaviour ascribed to both electrons and ions movements. This resistor generates a positive or negative overpotential η , depending on the current flow.

There are several experimental techniques for the estimation of the equivalent dc resistance
R
η
. Most of them are suitable for in-situ applications.

The easiest way to determine
R
η
is through a pulsed current test, as shown in the flow diagram in Fig. 18(a). This technique is recognised by the international standard ISO12405-4:2018 as the reference approach to estimate the internal dc resistance of the battery. It consists of a sequence of a discharge and charge pulse. A sudden voltage drop appears across the battery terminals in correspondence of the pulse fronts. The ratio
(33)
R =
Δ U
Δ I

Δ U
and
Δ I
are the voltage drop and the current pulse amplitude.

Schmalstieg et al. found that this resistance has two terms, a constant element and a temperature-dependant element, and it can be defined by using an Arrhenius relationship:
(34)

The Fig. 19 shows an example of a pulsed current test and the corresponding internal irreversible heat generation of a lithium-ion battery. A decrease of the heat generation during the test should occur, according to the temperature dependence of
R

.

The international standard IEC61960-3:2017-02 proposes an alternative to this technique, illustrated in Fig. 18(b). The battery should be discharged for 10s at 1C current rate, at the end of this period the terminal voltage

Although the standard suggests measuring the resistance after 1s, some papers prove that the resistance value highly depends on the measurement duration due to the physical and electrochemical processes. By comparing the measurement at 10 ms and 2s the value could change up to 30%.

Other techniques could be applied for the pure Ohmic resistance measurement such as the ac resistance using a sinusoidal signal at 1kHz [Fig. 18(c)]. This approach is described in the international standard IEC61960-3:2017-02 as well. The resulting resistance is given by:
(36)

where

are the rms values of battery voltage and current at 1kHz. Also in this case, the measurement could be dominated by inductive and capacitive behaviours that affect battery impedance at different frequencies, depending on battery technology and capacity. For this reason, the resistance measurements on the same battery rarely match when different techniques are used.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) may help to identify and separate all the individual resistance contributions of the Ohmic, charge transfer and diffusion phenomenon. The procedure for EIS is described with a flow chart in Fig. 18(d). It consists of measuring the voltage response
u
( t )
of the battery at sinusoidal current signals
i
( t )

f

. The voltage and current signals of the battery are transformed in the frequency domain and the impedance spectrum
Z
( f )

(37)

( f )
=
U
( f )
I
( f )
.
In a first approximation, the most suitable point corresponding to the pure ohmic impedance is the point with an imaginary part equal to zero, or the point corresponding to the minimum of the real part of the impedance. But in both cases, we are neglecting the high-frequency inductive behaviour that may change the frequency of these points according to the battery state. Therefore, the most accurate technique is to fit the EIS with an ECM to find the value of the pure ohmic resistance [99]. Very low frequencies are unnecessary for ohmic resistance determination, and 1Hz as a lower limit is enough.
Barai et al. showed that the total resistance measurements with different techniques perfectly match when time scales match, as it is shown in Fig. 20 [100].

It is not possible to select the best technique for the ohmic resistance measurement, the most suitable approach should be chosen according to the application and the test equipment available. The irreversible heating is affected by multiple equivalent resistances that do not depend only on the resistivity of electrodes. Even charge transfer resistance and ionic diffusion resistance may affect irreversible heat [101]. The entity of these contributions highly depends on the working frequency. Therefore, the acquisition of the overvoltage in the time-domain, or the acquisition of the equivalent impedance in the frequency-domain should be performed according to the battery dynamic during the expected normal operation conditions. In this framework EIS may be very effective.

The reversible heat flow of the battery is dependent on both the battery cathode and anode EHCs. Battery EHCs are influenced by SOC and they have a hysteresis behaviour depending on the applied charging or discharging current. Reversible heat generation can be positive or negative varying the SOC, indeed, it depends on lithium intercalation and several reactions occurring during battery cycling that could be endothermic or exothermic reactions.

Literature provides many different methods for EHC determination. The methods differ in their accuracy, measurement time and overall measurement complexity, and they can be classified according to the kind of measurement they require, which could be time-domain or frequency-domain.

The most used and well-established approaches for EHC determination are implemented in the time-domain, measuring the EHC at constant SOC and thermal equilibrium state [102]. Among them, potentiometric and calorimetric approaches are the most reliable and efficient. A detailed description of such approaches is presented in the following.
The potentiometric method, first introduced by Thompson [103], determines the EHC by measuring the change in the OCV as a function of temperature at fixed SOC values.

To perform the potentiometric method a high-resolution voltage measurement device and a climate chamber are needed [103]. The entire procedure is described by the flow chart in Fig. 21 (a). Starting from
SOC
0
= 0 %
and an initial temperature
T
0
, the open circuit voltage
U
OC
is measured in a predefined temperature range increasing the temperature in steps of
Δ T
. After every temperature increase
U
OC
is measured after a relaxing time to reach the thermal equilibrium state. Such a time period could last several hours depending on the entity of the temperature step, and the size of the battery. It is due to the slow reaction of the open circuit voltage to the temperature variations. After every temperature step, we can compute the EHC as:
(38)
Δ S
|
SOC
i
= n F
Δ
U
OC
Δ T
.
Repeating this procedure for several SOC levels between 0% and 100% we can use (30) to compute the entropic heat generation at different temperatures and different SOC.

The discharging rates, relaxation times, and the number of temperatures and SOC values should be chosen according to the application, and these parameters highly influence the time duration of the experiment which could last several weeks.
To sum up, this method provides accurate results, but with a remarkably long measuring time due to the relaxation phases.

A variation of the measurement protocol of the potentiometric method is a dynamic approach proposed by Osswald et al. shown in the flow chart in Fig. 21 (b). It is very similar to the previous approach, with the only difference that the temperature response of the OCV is measured during the potential relaxation towards the equilibrium. The authors identify the background voltage drift during relaxation, using a fitting algorithm, and then subtract it from the OCV measurement. With this approach, they reduce drastically the testing time. The EHC measurement time over 40 SOC values and 3 temperatures was reduced from 6 weeks to 4 days [104].

Lin et al. further reduce the relaxation time by using a depolarisation positive current pulse adjustment. This approach is shown in Fig. 21 (c). It remains similar to the dynamic potentiometric method, except for an additional step in the procedure. They state that the relaxation time is dominated by two different phenomena, the activation polarisation generated by the electrons accumulated in the proximity of the electrode surface, and the concentration polarisation generated by lithium ions diffusion in the electrolyte and electrode. The OCV slope during early relaxation is dominated by the activation polarisation. It is possible to accelerate the activation depolarisation by applying a small positive current pulse after the discharge pulse required to change the SOC in the potentiometric approach. [105].

The voltage relaxation dynamic becomes faster, and, by using also the background voltage drift subtraction, it is possible to reduce the relaxation time from the 90 min reported by Osswald et al. to approximately 10 min [106].

Quasi-equilibrium methods, like the potentiometric method, determine the EHC at a constant SOC, while the calorimetric method is a variable SOC method, and so provides a continuously entropic heat coefficient profile without relaxation times within the measuring process. This implies that the battery is constantly charged or discharged during the measurements by a direct current [107]. The complete procedure is summarised in the flow chart in Fig. 21(d). This method requires a calorimeter and a thermal chamber. The battery is placed in the calorimeter at a constant ambient temperature and is fully charged at low C-rate to 100% and back to 0% after a relaxation time of 2 h. During charging and discharging steps the battery temperature and voltage are measured. The calorimeter measures the total generated heat, including reversible and irreversible heat, during the charging and discharging process. Nevertheless, the reversible heat flow is the dominating heating source [103] when charging the battery at low C-Rates (0.1C and 0.05C in [103]), which is likely to obtain good EHC values by using (30). In conclusion, the calorimetric method delivers accurate results for the entropic heat coefficient within a short measuring time but requires expensive measurement equipment.

In alternative to time-domain methods, it is possible to use alternating current signals that allow us to determine the reversible heat generation in the frequency-domain saving time and helping to better discern irreversible and reversible heat rates.

Geng et al. [103] and Schmidt et al. [108] present methods to determine the entropic heat coefficient applying ac current stimulus to the battery cell. Both methods use Fourier analysis on the total generated heat to separate the reversible from the irreversible heat. This method is graphically shown in Fig. 22(a).
A square wave current stimulus is applied for a period of 4 h at the first SOC level to reach a thermal quasi-equilibrium.

A zero offset square wave current is used to charge and discharge within 10% SOC interval steps. After a warm-up of the battery due to Joule effect, the EHC could be obtained from the amplitude of the temperature swing
Δ T
and the peak-to-peak voltage fluctuations

To sum up, the alternating current method only uses simple measurement equipment at low costs and takes a total of 16 h for 9 SOC intervals between 5% and 95%, which is very fast compared to the potentiometric method.

Short measurement time and high accuracy are desirable for the EHC determination. Schmidt et al. proposed the use of electrothermal impedance spectroscopy (ETIS). This approach requires only a current source able to drive a sinusoidal current, a temperature sensor and a thermal chamber to control the ambient temperature [108].

This approach does not require a relaxation time, and therefore, by applying a small dc current and periodically measuring the EHC, it is possible to track the EHC trend with SOC in charge and discharge mode. This approach can show the hysteretic behaviour of the EHC.

The measurements of Schmidt et al. are in good agreement with the ones obtained with a potentiometric approach, but the time required for ETIS is more than one hundred times faster than the potentiometric approach with 10.8 SOC points per hour measured in [108], against 0.05 SOC points achieved with a potentiometric method.

ETIS method was improved by Abbasalnejad et al. including a physics-based electrochemical model. In this way, the entropy measurements are physically coupled to the internal state changes of the battery. The proposed method increases the complexity, but it may help to track the battery’s internal states [110].

Among hybrid time/frequency-domain methods, the two-step procedure called Hybridized Time-Frequency Domain Analysis, proposed by Hu et al. in [109], is particularly effective. Their approach combines the time- and frequency-domain analysis for the EHC determination, .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion, current challenges and future perspectives
ce:section.ce:para: Battery thermal modelling and characterisation play a crucial role for an optimal thermal management and temperature prediction in battery packs. It helps to optimise the efficiency, enhance the performance, and improve safety. Table 6 compares various battery thermal models reviewed in this article, highlighting their strengths (pros) and weaknesses (cons). Physics-based models offer the highest accuracy due to their detailed electrochemical and thermal representations. However, their computational burden makes them impractical for real-time applications. ECMs are a good compromise between accuracy and computational burden, making them ideal for integration into BMS. However, they require empirical parameter fitting, which limits their capability to extend the model to similar cases. Data-driven models are promising for applications where physical understanding is limited or not of interest. Another key point influencing the accuracy and the performance of the model is whether it is lumped or distributed. On the one hand, a distributed model is more precise in the spatial distribution of temperature, which is crucial for large battery packs. However, its high computational cost limits their practical use. On the other hand, lumped models are computationally efficient, but less accurate, as they assume a uniform temperature distribution.

The characterisation approaches of such thermal models differ among them for accuracy, cost, and real-time applicability. Ex-situ methods are ideal for research and precise thermal property measurement but not suitable for real battery applications. On the contrary, in-situ methods offer practical solutions for real-time monitoring, often based on predictive approaches that improve the estimation accuracy but require careful parameter tuning.

Battery thermal modelling and characterisation is currently a hot topic in lithium-ion battery research. In the following, we provide an overview of the main challenges and future research trends aimed to optimise battery thermal management.

Above the optimal design of active or passive cooling systems for BTMSs [2], the research in battery technologies remains a key point. It may help in identifying materials with better thermal properties, in terms of low internal heat generation and low temperature dependant rate for side reactions. Such materials could help not only in improving battery thermal performance and safety [111] but also in determining less stringent constraints for near-optimal operating conditions [112]. Higher ionic conductivity of the electrolyte, and lower resistance of the electrodes, together with an optimal design of electrode tabs and caps should help to reduce internal heat generation and improve temperature distribution [82]. Material engineering also helps with ageing retainment. Implementing self-healing features into batteries reduces degradation processes and could potentially restore damages in battery components, therefore it is a flourishing research field oriented to the development of a new generation of batteries [113].

Higher thermal conductivity of cell materials and large areas for positive and negative contacts may increase the temperature dissipation rate, making easier the fulfilment of Biot’s Eq. (5) and then allowing the use of simple lumped model rather than distributed model with lots of variables.

The development of in-situ approaches for battery thermal characterisation is a milestone in lithium battery research. It allows an effective application of optimal thermal management in industrial and commercial applications preserving performance and ensuring safety and a long life span.

EHC is the most difficult parameter to compute or estimate in-situ, because most of the characterisation approaches require the control of the environment temperature. Therefore, the development of an indirect fitting algorithm or controlling the ambient temperature with an active heater/cooler of the BTMS could be a solution but it still remains an unexplored field.

Nowadays, battery packs have sparse temperature sensing not consenting to the temperature measurement of each cell. In this context, effective battery thermal characterisation and modelling are fundamental. Additionally, the model fidelity, computational complexity, and parameterisation difficulty of thermal models should be well-traded to facilitate online applications [7]. Data-driven approaches may help in obtaining an accurate view of the temperature distribution across the battery pack, nevertheless, they require a huge computational burden and a large training dataset.

The limited processing resources available onboard a BMS restrict the use of physics-based models or distributed models, especially in large battery packs. Lumped ECM models are the most suitable for onboard applications, and they can easily be characterised with simple tests and without special laboratory equipment. These parameters could change with battery ageing, and for this reason, the models should periodically be characterised by refreshing the parameters.

This work presents a review of cell-scale thermal models for Lithium-ion batteries. Several families are identified for the classification of the main types of battery thermal models that describe the temperature distribution within the cell. The most common characterisation approaches are presented with an emphasis on in-situ techniques that can be integrated onboard. Heat dissipation and generation sources are described, and the main estimation techniques are discussed, allowing for the calculation of their contribution to the overall temperature distribution within the cell. In this context, we selected approaches that do not require specialised laboratory equipment and are suitable for onboard integration. The only exception is for the entropic heat coefficient, where traditional methods such as potentiometric and calorimetric approaches remain the most effective for an accurate estimation. Finally, future research trends in battery thermal management are explored, highlighting the main challenges that drive the design of the next generation of battery packs, from material design to overall thermal management systems.

Luigi Mattia: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hamzeh Beiranvand: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Walter Zamboni: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
